hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'viae' — 'viis' is dative/ablative plural
hints.Dative: Dative plural has the same form, but 'viis' here expresses means (ablative of means)
hints.Genitive: Genitive plural would be 'viarum' — 'viis' is ablative plural

hints.2nd: 2nd declension ablative plural ends in -is — 'via, viae' is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension ablative plural ends in -ibus — 'viis' with -is is 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension ablative plural ends in -ebus — 'via' is 1st declension

hints.to prove: 'to prove' (active) is 'probare' — 'probari' with -ri ending is passive infinitive
hints.to test: 'to test' is 'temptare' or 'probare' in another sense — 'probari' here means 'to be proved'
hints.to approve: 'to approve' is 'approbare' — 'probari' here means 'to be demonstrated'

hints.Present active infinitive: Active infinitive would be 'probare' — 'probari' with -ri is passive
hints.Perfect passive infinitive: Perfect passive infinitive would be 'probatum esse' — 'probari' is present passive
hints.Future passive infinitive: Future passive infinitive would be 'probatum iri' — 'probari' is present passive

hints.praeeo, praeire, praeivi, praeitum: These are the parts of 'praeire' (to go before) — a different verb
hints.prohibeo, prohibere, prohibui, prohibitu: These are the parts of 'prohibere' (to prevent) — do not confuse with 'probare'
hints.procedo, procedere, processi, processum: These are the parts of 'procedere' (to proceed) — a different verb

hints.to be powerful: 'to be powerful' might suggest 'pollere' — 'posse' specifically means 'to be able'

hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'possit' — 'potest' is indicative
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'poterit' — 'potest' is present indicative

hints.pono, ponere, posui, positum: These are the parts of 'ponere' (to place) — not 'posse'
hints.potior, potiri, potitus sum: These are the parts of 'potiri' (to gain possession of) — a different verb
hints.polliceor, polliceri, pollicitus sum: These are the parts of 'polliceri' (to promise) — not related

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' (sometimes 'esse' in archaic forms) — here 'esse' means 'to be'
hints.to go: 'to go' is 'ire' — 'esse' is the infinitive of 'sum'

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative 3rd singular is 'est' — 'esse' is the infinitive
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'esse' is the infinitive form
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect is 'erat' — 'esse' is the present infinitive

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — not related to 'esse' (to be)
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go) — not related
hints.existo, existere, extiti: These are the parts of 'existere' (to emerge/exist) — 'esse' is the basic verb 'to be'

steps.prompt: What does 'Deum esse' function as in this sentence?

hints.A purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'Deum esse' is indirect statement (acc. + inf.)
hints.A result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — this is an accusative-infinitive construction
hints.A conditional protasis: Conditionals use 'si' — 'Deum esse' is an indirect statement governed by 'probari potest'

translation: That God exists can be proved in five ways.
